mechanism: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Bacillus Calmette Guerin (BCG) (Intravesical).
clinical_effect: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Bacillus Calmette Guerin (BCG) (Intravesical).
management: Avoid concomitant use of intravesical BCG and antibiotics, particularly those given via intravenous or oral routes and those with activity against mycobacteria.

mechanism: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Cholera Vaccine.
clinical_effect: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Cholera Vaccine.
management: Avoid cholera vaccine in patients receiving systemic antibiotics, and within 14 days following the use of oral or parenteral antibiotics.

mechanism: Alcohol (Ethyl) may increase CNS depressant effects of Opioid Agonists.
clinical_effect: Alcohol (Ethyl) may increase CNS depressant effects of Opioid Agonists.
management: Avoid alcohol consumption during treatment with opioids. Inform patients and caregivers that potentially fatal additive effects may occur if opioids are combined with alcohol. Consider prescribing an opioid reversal agent for the emergency treatment of opioid overdose.

mechanism: Bromperidol may decrease hypotensive effects of Blood Pressure Lowering Agents. Blood Pressure Lowering Agents may increase hypotensive effects of Bromperidol.
clinical_effect: Bromperidol may decrease hypotensive effects of Blood Pressure Lowering Agents. Blood Pressure Lowering Agents may increase hypotensive effects of Bromperidol.
management: Avoid the concomitant use of bromperidol and blood pressure lowering agents.

mechanism: Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase adverse/toxic effects of Morphine (Systemic).
clinical_effect: Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase adverse/toxic effects of Morphine (Systemic).
management: Do not use morphine in patients taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) or within 14 days of MAOI discontinuation. Morphine prescribing information lists this combination as contraindicated.

mechanism: OAT1/3 Inhibitors may increase serum concentrations of Seladelpar.
clinical_effect: OAT1/3 Inhibitors may increase serum concentrations of Seladelpar.
management: Avoid concomitant use of seladelpar and OAT1/3 inhibitors.

mechanism: Ergot Derivatives (Vasoconstrictive CYP3A4 Substrates) may increase vasoconstricting effects of Alpha-/Beta-Agonists.
clinical_effect: Ergot Derivatives (Vasoconstrictive CYP3A4 Substrates) may increase vasoconstricting effects of Alpha-/Beta-Agonists.
management: Avoid concomitant use of these vasoconstricting ergot derivatives with alpha-/beta-agonists, including those used in regional anesthesia, due to a risk of significant blood pressure elevations.

mechanism: Ergot Derivatives (Vasoconstrictive CYP3A4 Substrates) may increase vasoconstricting effects of Alpha-/Beta-Agonists.
clinical_effect: Ergot Derivatives (Vasoconstrictive CYP3A4 Substrates) may increase vasoconstricting effects of Alpha-/Beta-Agonists.
management: Avoid concomitant use of these vasoconstricting ergot derivatives with alpha-/beta-agonists, including those used in regional anesthesia, due to a risk of significant blood pressure elevations.

mechanism: Kratom may increase adverse/toxic effects of Sympathomimetics.
clinical_effect: Kratom may increase adverse/toxic effects of Sympathomimetics.
management: Use of kratom together with a sympathomimetic is not recommended. If used concurrently, monitor for increased effects of sympathomimetics (eg, blood pressure, heart rate), seizure, and other signs of toxicity.

mechanism: QuiNINE may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: QuiNINE may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: Avoid concurrent use of quinine and neuromuscular blockers.

mechanism: QuiNINE may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: QuiNINE may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: Avoid concurrent use of quinine and neuromuscular blockers.

mechanism: Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase adverse/toxic effects of Apraclonidine. Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase serum concentrations of Apraclonidine.
clinical_effect: Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase adverse/toxic effects of Apraclonidine. Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase serum concentrations of Apraclonidine.
management: Do not use apraclonidine in patients treated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). Apraclonidine prescribing information lists such use as contraindicated.

management: Avoid cholera vaccine in patients receiving systemic antibiotics, and within 14 days following the use of oral or parenteral antibiotics.

mechanism: Dapoxetine may increase serotonergic effects of Serotonergic Agents (High Risk). This could result in serotonin syndrome.
clinical_effect: Dapoxetine may increase serotonergic effects of Serotonergic Agents (High Risk). This could result in serotonin syndrome.
management: Do not use serotonergic agents (high risk) with dapoxetine or within 7 days of serotonergic agent discontinuation. Do not use dapoxetine within 14 days of monoamine oxidase inhibitor use. Dapoxetine labeling lists this combination as contraindicated.

mechanism: Agents With Seizure Threshold Lowering Potential may increase adverse/toxic effects of Pipamperone. Specifically, the risk of seizures may be increased.
clinical_effect: Agents With Seizure Threshold Lowering Potential may increase adverse/toxic effects of Pipamperone. Specifically, the risk of seizures may be increased.
management: Avoid coadministration of pipamperone with agents that may lower the seizure threshold, as this may increase the risk of seizures.

mechanism: Photosensitizing Agents may increase photosensitizing effects of Aminolevulinic Acid (Systemic).
clinical_effect: Photosensitizing Agents may increase photosensitizing effects of Aminolevulinic Acid (Systemic).
management: Avoid administration of other photosensitizing agents within 24 hours before or 24 hours after orally administered aminolevulinic acid.

mechanism: Photosensitizing Agents may increase photosensitizing effects of Aminolevulinic Acid (Systemic).
clinical_effect: Photosensitizing Agents may increase photosensitizing effects of Aminolevulinic Acid (Systemic).
management: Avoid administration of other photosensitizing agents within 24 hours before or 24 hours after orally administered aminolevulinic acid.

mechanism: Bradycardia-Causing Agents may increase bradycardic effects of Landiolol.
clinical_effect: Bradycardia-Causing Agents may increase bradycardic effects of Landiolol.
management: Avoid concomitant use of landiolol with other agents that cause bradycardia.

mechanism: Bradycardia-Causing Agents may increase bradycardic effects of Landiolol.
clinical_effect: Bradycardia-Causing Agents may increase bradycardic effects of Landiolol.
management: Avoid concomitant use of landiolol with other agents that cause bradycardia.

mechanism: Lefamulin may increase QTc-prolonging effects of QT-prolonging CYP3A4 Substrates.
clinical_effect: Lefamulin may increase QTc-prolonging effects of QT-prolonging CYP3A4 Substrates.
management: Do not use lefamulin tablets with QT-prolonging CYP3A4 substrates. Lefamulin prescribing information lists this combination as contraindicated.

mechanism: MetroNIDAZOLE (Systemic) may increase adverse/toxic effects of Products Containing Propylene Glycol. A disulfiram-like reaction may occur.
clinical_effect: MetroNIDAZOLE (Systemic) may increase adverse/toxic effects of Products Containing Propylene Glycol. A disulfiram-like reaction may occur.
management: Use of alcohol or propylene glycol with metronidazole or within 3 days after stopping metronidazole is a listed contraindication in the metronidazole prescribing information. The specific concern is that a disulfiram-like reaction could result from coadministration. mechanism: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Bacillus Calmette Guerin (BCG) (Intravesical).
clinical_effect: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Bacillus Calmette Guerin (BCG) (Intravesical).
management: Avoid concomitant use of intravesical BCG and antibiotics, particularly those given via intravenous or oral routes and those with activity against mycobacteria.

mechanism: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Cholera Vaccine.
clinical_effect: Antibiotics may decrease therapeutic effects of Cholera Vaccine.
management: Avoid cholera vaccine in patients receiving systemic antibiotics, and within 14 days following the use of oral or parenteral antibiotics.

mechanism: Alcohol (Ethyl) may increase CNS depressant effects of Opioid Agonists.
clinical_effect: Alcohol (Ethyl) may increase CNS depressant effects of Opioid Agonists.
management: Avoid alcohol consumption during treatment with opioids. Inform patients and caregivers that potentially fatal additive effects may occur if opioids are combined with alcohol. Consider prescribing an opioid reversal agent for the emergency treatment of opioid overdose.

mechanism: Alcohol (Ethyl) may increase CNS depressant effects of Opioid Agonists.
clinical_effect: Alcohol (Ethyl) may increase CNS depressant effects of Opioid Agonists.
management: Avoid alcohol consumption during treatment with opioids. Inform patients and caregivers that potentially fatal additive effects may occur if opioids are combined with alcohol. Consider prescribing an opioid reversal agent for the emergency treatment of opioid overdose.

mechanism: Azelastine (Nasal) may increase CNS depressant effects of CNS Depressants.
clinical_effect: Azelastine (Nasal) may increase CNS depressant effects of CNS Depressants.
management: Avoid combining nasal azelastine with other CNS depressants due to the potential for enhanced CNS depressant effects.

clinical_effect: Bradycardia-Causing Agents may increase arrhythmogenic effects of Fexinidazole.
management: Avoid concomitant use of fexinidazole with bradycardia-causing agents. mechanism: Abciximab may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists.
clinical_effect: Abciximab may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists.
management: Do not use abciximab in patients treated with vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) or who have received such agents in the past 7 days, unless the prothrombin time is 1.2 times control or less. Abciximab product labeling lists this combination as contraindicated.

mechanism: Alteplase may increase anticoagulant effects of Anticoagulants.
clinical_effect: Alteplase may increase anticoagulant effects of Anticoagulants.
management: For the treatment of acute ischemic stroke (AIS): avoid use of alteplase in patients with recent receipt of anticoagulants. For patients treated with vitamin K antagonists: avoid alteplase if INR is above 1.7. For patients taking direct factor Xa or thrombin inhibitors: avoid alteplase unless laboratory results (eg, aPTT, INR, platelets, ecarin clotting time, thrombin time, appropriate anti-factor

mechanism: Alteplase may increase anticoagulant effects of Anticoagulants.
clinical_effect: Alteplase may increase anticoagulant effects of Anticoagulants.
management: For the treatment of acute ischemic stroke (AIS): avoid use of alteplase in patients with recent receipt of anticoagulants. For patients treated with vitamin K antagonists: avoid alteplase if INR is above 1.7. mechanism: Mefloquine may decrease therapeutic effects of Antiseizure Agents. Mefloquine may decrease serum concentrations of Antiseizure Agents.
clinical_effect: Mefloquine may decrease therapeutic effects of Antiseizure Agents. Mefloquine may decrease serum concentrations of Antiseizure Agents.
management: If an antiseizure drug is being used to treat or prevent seizures, avoid using mefloquine when possible, as mefloquine is contraindicated for malaria prophylaxis (not treatment) in persons with a history of seizures. If an antiseizure drug is being used for another indication (eg, treatment of neuropathic pain, mood stabilization, etc.), monitor antiseizure serum concentrations and response to tre

mechanism: Calcium Salts (Intravenous) may increase adverse/toxic effects of CefTRIAXone. Ceftriaxone binds to calcium forming an insoluble precipitate.
clinical_effect: Calcium Salts (Intravenous) may increase adverse/toxic effects of CefTRIAXone. Ceftriaxone binds to calcium forming an insoluble precipitate.
management: Use of ceftriaxone is contraindicated in neonates (28 days of age or younger) who require (or are expected to require) treatment with IV calcium-containing solutions. In other patients, these solutions can be used sequentially if the infusion lines are flushed with a compatible fluid between ceftriaxone and calcium-containing solution infusion.

mechanism: Morphine (Systemic) may decrease antiplatelet effects of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors). Morphine (Systemic) may decrease serum concentrations of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Morphine (Systemic) may decrease antiplatelet effects of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors). Morphine (Systemic) may decrease serum concentrations of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors).
management: Morphine may reduce the antiplatelet effects of P2Y12 inhibitors. The clinical risk associated with this interaction has not been fully investigated and may vary with dose, regimen, timing, patient population, concomitant pharmacotherapy, or other factors. Patients with acute coronary syndromes are treated simultaneously with multiple antiplatelet agents (eg, aspirin, glycoprotein Iib/IIIa recepto

mechanism: Drugs that Affect Gallbladder Function may decrease therapeutic effects of Sincalide.
clinical_effect: Drugs that Affect Gallbladder Function may decrease therapeutic effects of Sincalide.
management: Consider discontinuing drugs that may affect gallbladder motility prior to the use of sincalide to stimulate gallbladder contraction.

mechanism: Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase hypertensive effects of DOPamine.
clinical_effect: Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors may increase hypertensive effects of DOPamine.
management: Initiate dopamine at no greater than one-tenth (1/10) of the usual dose in patients who are taking (or have taken within the last 2 to 3 weeks) monoamine oxidase inhibitors. Monitor for an exaggerated hypertensive response to dopamine.

mechanism: Colistimethate may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: Colistimethate may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: If possible, avoid concomitant use of these products. Monitor for deeper, prolonged neuromuscular-blocking effects (respiratory paralysis) in patients receiving concomitant neuromuscular-blocking agents and colistimethate.

mechanism: Colistimethate may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: Colistimethate may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: If possible, avoid concomitant use of these products. Monitor for deeper, prolonged neuromuscular-blocking effects (respiratory paralysis) in patients receiving concomitant neuromuscular-blocking agents and colistimethate.

mechanism: Linezolid may increase hypertensive effects of Sympathomimetics.
clinical_effect: Linezolid may increase hypertensive effects of Sympathomimetics.
management: Consider initial dose reductions of sympathomimetic agents, and closely monitor for enhanced blood pressure elevations, in patients receiving linezolid.

mechanism: Linezolid may increase hypertensive effects of Sympathomimetics.
clinical_effect: Linezolid may increase hypertensive effects of Sympathomimetics.
management: Consider initial dose reductions of sympathomimetic agents, and closely monitor for enhanced blood pressure elevations, in patients receiving linezolid.

mechanism: Amiodarone may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists. Amiodarone may increase serum concentrations of Vitamin K Antagonists.
clinical_effect: Amiodarone may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists. Amiodarone may increase serum concentrations of Vitamin K Antagonists.
management: Monitor patients extra closely for evidence of increased anticoagulant effects if amiodarone is initiated/dose increased, or decreased effects if amiodarone is discontinued/dose decreased, though due to the long half-life of amiodarone, any interaction may persist for several days/weeks following any dose decrease or discontinuation. An empiric warfarin dosage reduction of 30% to 50% at the initia

mechanism: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
management: Consider avoiding this combination if possible. Monitor patients treated with fusidic acid and these CYP3A4 substrates closely for increased adverse effects during any combined use. Use of such a combination may increase the risk for toxicity from the CYP3A4 substrate.

mechanism: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
management: Consider avoiding this combination if possible. Monitor patients treated with fusidic acid and these CYP3A4 substrates closely for increased adverse effects during any combined use. Use of such a combination may increase the risk for toxicity from the CYP3A4 substrate.

mechanism: CYP3A4 Inhibitors (Moderate) may increase serum concentrations of FentaNYL.
clinical_effect: CYP3A4 Inhibitors (Moderate) may increase serum concentrations of FentaNYL.
management: A fentanyl dose reduction should be considered especially when a CYP3A4 inhibitor is added to patients receiving fentanyl until the effects of the combination are known. Monitor for respiratory depression and sedation. Upon discontinuation of a CYP3A4 inhibitor, a fentanyl dose increase may be considered. Monitor for signs and symptoms of withdrawal.

mechanism: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
management: Consider avoiding this combination if possible. Monitor patients treated with fusidic acid and these CYP3A4 substrates closely for increased adverse effects during any combined use. Use of such a combination may increase the risk for toxicity from the CYP3A4 substrate.

mechanism: CYP3A4 Inhibitors (Moderate) may increase serum concentrations of Midazolam.
clinical_effect: CYP3A4 Inhibitors (Moderate) may increase serum concentrations of Midazolam.
management: Avoid concomitant use of nasal midazolam and moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors. Consider alternatives to use with oral midazolam whenever possible and consider using lower midazolam doses. Monitor patients closely for increased and prolonged midazolam effects and toxicities (eg, sedation, respiratory depression) if these agents are combined.

mechanism: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
management: Consider avoiding this combination if possible. Monitor patients treated with fusidic acid and these CYP3A4 substrates closely for increased adverse effects during any combined use. Use of such a combination may increase the risk for toxicity from the CYP3A4 substrate.

mechanism: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Fusidic Acid (Systemic) may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
management: Consider avoiding this combination if possible. Monitor patients treated with fusidic acid and these CYP3A4 substrates closely for increased adverse effects during any combined use. Use of such a combination may increase the risk for toxicity from the CYP3A4 substrate.

mechanism: Drugs that Affect Gallbladder Function may decrease therapeutic effects of Sincalide.
clinical_effect: Drugs that Affect Gallbladder Function may decrease therapeutic effects of Sincalide.
management: Consider discontinuing drugs that may affect gallbladder motility prior to the use of sincalide to stimulate gallbladder contraction.

mechanism: Drugs that Affect Gallbladder Function may decrease therapeutic effects of Sincalide.
clinical_effect: Drugs that Affect Gallbladder Function may decrease therapeutic effects of Sincalide.
management: Consider discontinuing drugs that may affect gallbladder motility prior to the use of sincalide to stimulate gallbladder contraction.

mechanism: Hyponatremia-Associated Agents may increase hyponatremic effects of Desmopressin.
clinical_effect: Hyponatremia-Associated Agents may increase hyponatremic effects of Desmopressin.
management: Monitor serum sodium and for signs of water intoxication or hyponatremia more closely in patients receiving concomitant hyponatremia-associated agents with desmopressin.

mechanism: Hyponatremia-Associated Agents may increase hyponatremic effects of Desmopressin.
clinical_effect: Hyponatremia-Associated Agents may increase hyponatremic effects of Desmopressin.
management: Monitor serum sodium and for signs of water intoxication or hyponatremia more closely in patients receiving concomitant hyponatremia-associated agents with desmopressin.

mechanism: QT-prolonging Agents (Indeterminate Risk - Caution) may increase QTc-prolonging effects of Haloperidol.
clinical_effect: QT-prolonging Agents (Indeterminate Risk - Caution) may increase QTc-prolonging effects of Haloperidol.
management: Monitor for QTc interval prolongation and ventricular arrhythmias (including torsades de pointes) when these drugs are combined. Patients with other risk factors (eg, older age, female sex, bradycardia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, heart disease, and higher drug concentrations) are likely at greater risk for these potentially life-threatening toxicities.

mechanism: QT-prolonging Agents (Indeterminate Risk - Caution) may increase QTc-prolonging effects of QT-prolonging Agents (Highest Risk).
clinical_effect: QT-prolonging Agents (Indeterminate Risk - Caution) may increase QTc-prolonging effects of QT-prolonging Agents (Highest Risk).
management: Monitor for QTc interval prolongation and ventricular arrhythmias (including torsades de pointes) when these drugs are combined. Patients with other risk factors (eg, older age, female sex, bradycardia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, heart disease, and higher drug concentrations) are likely at greater risk for these potentially life-threatening toxicities.

mechanism: Piperacillin may increase hypokalemic effects of Diuretics.
clinical_effect: Piperacillin may increase hypokalemic effects of Diuretics.
management: Consider monitoring serum potassium in patients treated with piperacillin and diuretics, as they may be at increased risk of hypokalemia.

mechanism: DOPamine may increase hypotensive effects of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
clinical_effect: DOPamine may increase hypotensive effects of Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin.
management: Consider monitoring for decreased blood pressure in patients receiving intravenous phenytoin. Hemodynamically unstable patients, including those requiring dopamine to maintain adequate blood pressure, are at elevated risk.

mechanism: Calcium Channel Blockers may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents (Nondepolarizing).
clinical_effect: Calcium Channel Blockers may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents (Nondepolarizing).
management: Monitor for reduced onset time, and enhanced or prolonged effects of nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockers in patients receiving calcium channel blockers.

mechanism: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents (Nondepolarizing). Specifically, this effect is observed with chronic phenytoin administration. Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents (Nondepolarizing). Specifically, this effects is observed with acute/short term phenytoin administration
clinical_effect: Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may decrease neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents (Nondepolarizing). Specifically, this effect is observed with chronic phenytoin administration. Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents (Nondepolarizing). Specifically, this effects is observed with acute/short term phenytoin administration
management: Monitor phenytoin-treated patients closely for altered response to neuromuscular-blocking agents. Patients receiving chronic phenytoin therapy (taking for more than 7 to 14 days) are expected to experience a diminished neuromuscular-blocking agent response. Patients should be monitored for more rapid recovery from neuromuscular blockade than expected, and neuromuscular blocker infusion rate requir

mechanism: Loop Diuretics may decrease neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents. Loop Diuretics may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: Loop Diuretics may decrease neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents. Loop Diuretics may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: Be aware that the therapeutic effects of neuromuscular blockers may be altered by coadministration of loop diuretics. Both increased and decreased effects of neuromuscular blocking agents have been observed.

mechanism: Magnesium Salts may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: Magnesium Salts may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: Monitor for enhanced or prolonged effects of neuromuscular blocking agents in patients receiving magnesium supplementation. High-risk patients include those with elevated serum magnesium concentrations or renal impairment and those receiving parenteral magnesium.

mechanism: Clofazimine may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Clofazimine may increase serum concentrations of CYP3A4 Substrates (High risk with Inhibitors).
management: Monitor for increased toxicities/effects of CYP3A4 substrates if combined with clofazimine.

mechanism: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease active metabolite exposure of Amiodarone. CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of Amiodarone.
clinical_effect: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease active metabolite exposure of Amiodarone. CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of Amiodarone.
management: Monitor for reduced amiodarone concentrations and effectiveness if combined with strong CYP3A4 inducers. Amiodarone dose increases may be required to maintain therapeutic effects. Maximal reductions in amiodarone exposure are expected to occur after multiple doses of the strong CYP3A4 inducer have been administered, within days to weeks of initiation. Induction effects may also persist for days to

mechanism: CYP3A4 Inhibitors (Moderate) may increase serum concentrations of Amiodarone.
clinical_effect: CYP3A4 Inhibitors (Moderate) may increase serum concentrations of Amiodarone.
management: Monitor for increased amiodarone concentrations and toxicities if combined with moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors.

mechanism: Beta-Blockers may decrease therapeutic effects of DOBUTamine.
clinical_effect: Beta-Blockers may decrease therapeutic effects of DOBUTamine.
management: Monitor for reduced dobutamine efficacy if used in patients taking, or who have recently taken, beta-blockers. Consider avoiding this combination when possible.

mechanism: Calcium Salts may decrease therapeutic effects of DOBUTamine.
clinical_effect: Calcium Salts may decrease therapeutic effects of DOBUTamine.
management: Monitor for decreased therapeutic effects of dobutamine (specifically cardiac output) if a calcium supplement (especially IV) is initiated/dose increased.

mechanism: CYP2C19 Inhibitors (Strong) may increase serum concentrations of DiazePAM.
clinical_effect: CYP2C19 Inhibitors (Strong) may increase serum concentrations of DiazePAM.
management: Monitor for increased and prolonged diazepam effects and toxicities (eg, somnolence, sedation) if combined with strong CYP2C19 inhibitors. Lorazepam and oxazepam do not rely on CYP metabolic pathways and may be acceptable alternatives to diazepam.

mechanism: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of DiazePAM.
clinical_effect: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of DiazePAM.
management: Monitor for reduced diazepam efficacy if combined with strong CYP3A4 inducers.

mechanism: Lumacaftor and Ivacaftor may decrease serum concentrations of CYP2C19 Substrates (High risk with Inducers).
clinical_effect: Lumacaftor and Ivacaftor may decrease serum concentrations of CYP2C19 Substrates (High risk with Inducers).
management: Monitor clinical response to any CYP2C19 substrates closely when used with lumacaftor/ivacaftor. Drugs inactivated by CYP2C19 may have decreased effectiveness, while any drugs activated by CYP2C19 may exhibit increased effects or toxicity.

mechanism: Lumacaftor and Ivacaftor may decrease serum concentrations of CYP2C19 Substrates (High risk with Inducers).
clinical_effect: Lumacaftor and Ivacaftor may decrease serum concentrations of CYP2C19 Substrates (High risk with Inducers).
management: Monitor clinical response to any CYP2C19 substrates closely when used with lumacaftor/ivacaftor. Drugs inactivated by CYP2C19 may have decreased effectiveness, while any drugs activated by CYP2C19 may exhibit increased effects or toxicity.

mechanism: Loop Diuretics may increase nephrotoxic effects of Vancomycin.
clinical_effect: Loop Diuretics may increase nephrotoxic effects of Vancomycin.
management: Monitor for nephrotoxic effects of vancomycin if a loop diuretic is initiated or the dose is increased. Vancomycin labeling recommends increased monitoring of renal function with concurrent use of other potentially nephrotoxic medications.

mechanism: Vancomycin may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: Vancomycin may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: Monitor for increased and/or prolonged therapeutic effects of neuromuscular-blocking agents (NMB), specifically respiratory depression, if combined with vancomycin.

mechanism: Vancomycin may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
clinical_effect: Vancomycin may increase neuromuscular-blocking effects of Neuromuscular-Blocking Agents.
management: Monitor for increased and/or prolonged therapeutic effects of neuromuscular-blocking agents (NMB), specifically respiratory depression, if combined with vancomycin.

mechanism: FentaNYL may decrease antiplatelet effects of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors). FentaNYL may decrease serum concentrations of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: FentaNYL may decrease antiplatelet effects of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors). FentaNYL may decrease serum concentrations of Antiplatelet Agents (P2Y12 Inhibitors).
management: The clinical risk associated with this interaction has not been fully investigated and may vary with dose, regimen, timing, patient population, concomitant pharmacotherapy, or other factors. Monitor patients with acute coronary syndromes who are treated simultaneously with multiple antiplatelet agents (eg, aspirin, glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor antagonists) and/or an anticoagulant (eg, bivalirudi

mechanism: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of FentaNYL.
clinical_effect: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of FentaNYL.
management: Monitor patient closely for evidence of reduced analgesic effects and/or emergence of possible withdrawal signs/symptoms if fentanyl is used together with a strong CYP3A4 inducer, particularly if the CYP3A4 inducer is initiated in a patient previously stable on fentanyl. Caution is also warranted if a patient who had been maintained on this combination discontinues the strong CYP3A4 inducer, as fe

mechanism: Aldesleukin may increase serum concentrations of CYP Substrates (Narrow Therapeutic Index/Sensitive with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Aldesleukin may increase serum concentrations of CYP Substrates (Narrow Therapeutic Index/Sensitive with Inhibitors).
management: Monitor for increased concentrations and effects of these narrow therapeutic index/sensitive CYP substrates when combined with aldesleukin.

mechanism: Aldesleukin may increase serum concentrations of CYP Substrates (Narrow Therapeutic Index/Sensitive with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Aldesleukin may increase serum concentrations of CYP Substrates (Narrow Therapeutic Index/Sensitive with Inhibitors).
management: Monitor for increased concentrations and effects of these narrow therapeutic index/sensitive CYP substrates when combined with aldesleukin.

mechanism: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of NiCARdipine.
clinical_effect: CYP3A4 Inducers (Strong) may decrease serum concentrations of NiCARdipine.
management: Monitor for reduced nicardipine efficacy when combined with strong CYP3A4 inducers.

mechanism: Acoramidis may increase serum concentrations of CYP2C9 Substrates (Narrow Therapeutic Index/Sensitive with Inhibitors).
clinical_effect: Acoramidis may increase serum concentrations of CYP2C9 Substrates (Narrow Therapeutic Index/Sensitive with Inhibitors).
management: Monitor for increased effects and toxicities of these CYP2C9 narrow therapeutic index/sensitive substrates when combined with acoramidis.

mechanism: Cephalosporins may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists.
clinical_effect: Cephalosporins may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists.
management: Monitor for elevated INR and bleeding if a vitamin K antagonist is used in combination with cephalosporins. Cephalosporins that have an NMTT (1-MTT) side chain in their chemical structure may pose the greatest risk of interaction.

mechanism: Penicillins may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists.
clinical_effect: Penicillins may increase anticoagulant effects of Vitamin K Antagonists.
management: Monitor anticoagulation status more closely in vitamin K antagonist-treated patients both during concurrent use of a penicillin antibiotic and for at least several days after cessation of the antibiotic.
